abstract:
This review summarizes recent advancements in the theoretical understanding of novel bonding modes for carbon and its heavier group 14 congeners (Si–Pb). Central to this discussion is the identification of tetrylones (EL₂), a class of divalent E(0) compounds—including carbones, silylones, and germylones—that are electronically distinct from traditional tetrylenes (ER₂). Unlike tetrylenes, which feature electron-sharing bonds and a single lone pair, tetrylones function as bidentate Lewis acids where a naked tetrele atom in the singlet ¹D state binds two σ-donor ligands through donor–acceptor interactions, thereby retaining two lone pairs. Charge- and energy decomposition analyses (EDA-NOCV) quantify these unique electronic structures, providing a rationale for their distinct chemical reactivity and supporting the expanding library of experimentally isolated species like carbodicarbenes and silylones.

The review further extends these bonding concepts to transition metal complexes [TM]–E featuring terminal, naked group 14 atoms. Bonding analyses reveal that these complexes are best described by a combination of σ and π_|_ electron-sharing bonds alongside π_|| back-donation, effectively characterizing the tetrele atom as a formal four-electron donor. Theoretical results emphasize the isolobal relationship between these carbon complexes and carbon monoxide, positioning them as potent ligands for auxiliary Lewis acids. By bridging the gap between fundamental electronic structure theory and synthetic chemistry, this work provides a comprehensive framework for predicting the stability and reactivity of as-yet unknown main group and transition metal tetrele species.
